methods.description: Conducting a biological survey in mountain ponds, classifying them based on hydroperiod, measuring key environmental variables in situ, and collecting periphytic diatom samples for laboratory analysis.

steps.description: During spring 2017 a biological survey was conducted (Junta de Andalucía approval no. SGYB/AF) in 45 mountain ponds, both temporary and permanent (median elevation: 1203 m a.s.l., range: 225-2520 m a.s.l.) located in Andalusia (south of Spain, Fig. 1).
steps.searchable_summary: conduct biological survey and select study sites (mountain ponds)
outputs: List of 45 selected pond locations

steps.description: We consider temporary ponds those that permanently dried every year, whereas permanent  ponds are those that do not dry for at least a decade (Chase, 2003).
steps.searchable_summary: classify ponds based on hydroperiod (temporary or permanent)
inputs: Observation or historical data on pond drying frequency for each selected pond
outputs: Classification of each pond as temporary or permanent

steps.description: turbidity ... were the unique environmental variables that were measured in situ
steps.searchable_summary: measure turbidity in situ at each pond
outputs: Turbidity value for each pond

steps.description: conductivity were the unique environmental variables that were measured in situ
steps.searchable_summary: measure conductivity in situ at each pond
outputs: Conductivity value for each pond

steps.description: Periphytic diatoms were collected from each system
steps.searchable_summary: collect periphytic diatom samples from each pond
outputs: Raw periphytic diatom samples from each pond

steps.description: samples were processed ... in the laboratory by following the method explained in Blanco & Becares (2006).
steps.searchable_summary: process collected diatom samples in the laboratory
outputs: Processed diatom samples ready for slide preparation

methods.description: Laboratory procedures for cleaning diatom frustules from processed samples, mounting them on permanent slides, and then identifying and counting individual diatoms under a microscope to determine taxonomic composition and abundance.

steps.description: Diatoms were cleaned ... on permanent microscope slides according to the European protocol EN14407.
steps.searchable_summary: clean diatoms from processed samples
outputs: Cleaned diatom frustules

steps.description: Diatoms were ... mounted on permanent microscope slides according to the European protocol EN14407.
steps.searchable_summary: mount cleaned diatoms on permanent microscope slides
outputs: Permanent microscope slides with mounted diatoms

steps.description: a homogeneous count of ca. 100 individuals (diatom valves) were identified on each slide
steps.searchable_summary: identify and count approximately 100 diatom individuals per slide
inputs: Permanent microscope slides with mounted diatoms
outputs: Count of diatom individuals per slide

steps.description: identified on each slide at the lowest possible taxonomic level following Hofmann, Werum & Lange-Bertalot (2011) and references therein.
steps.searchable_summary: identify diatoms to the lowest possible taxonomic level
inputs: Taxonomic keys and references (Hofmann, Werum & Lange-Bertalot 2011)
outputs: Taxonomic identification for each counted diatom individual

methods.description: Calculating ecological metrics such as community uniqueness and diversity from diatom abundance data, and spatial metrics like isolation based on pond locations. Also includes testing for spatial patterns like clustering and autocorrelation.

steps.description: Uniqueness of diatom communities(U ) of each pond i was calculated as: where A j and O j are the abundance (relative) and the occurrence (rescaled to the [0-1] range), respectively, of the jth taxon.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate diatom community uniqueness (U) for each pond
inputs: Diatom abundance data (relative) per taxon per pond
Diatom occurrence data (rescaled [0-1]) per taxon per pond
outputs: Uniqueness value (U) for each pond

steps.description: Diatom diversity was calculated using Shannon-Wiener index: H = − n i n ln n i n where n i /n is the proportion of the ith taxon in the sample.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate Shannon-Wiener diversity index (H) for each pond
inputs: Diatom abundance data (proportion of each taxon) per pond
outputs: Shannon-Wiener diversity index (H) for each pond

steps.description: Spatial clustering was tested with Nearest Neighbours analysis
steps.searchable_summary: test spatial clustering using Nearest Neighbours analysis
outputs: Results of Nearest Neighbours analysis (e.g., test statistic, p-value)

steps.description: spatial autocorrelation was tested using Moran's statistic.
steps.searchable_summary: test spatial autocorrelation using Moran's I statistic
inputs: Variable of interest (e.g., U, H, environmental data)
Spatial weights matrix (implicitly needed for Moran's I)
outputs: Moran's I statistic and significance

steps.description: the degree of isolation (I ) of each pond i was calculated with a Gaussian kernel density estimator (Hammer, Harper & Ryan, 2001): where d i is the Euclidean distance between pairs of locations, and r is the scale radius of the kernel, here set to 0.1.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate pond isolation using Gaussian kernel density estimator
outputs: Isolation value (I) for each pond

methods.description: Using statistical models (GLM ANCOVA) to analyze the relationship between diatom community metrics (diversity, uniqueness) and environmental/spatial factors, selecting the best model using AIC, and evaluating distance-decay relationships using Mantel tests.

steps.description: To analyse the response of the diatom diversity and the uniqueness of diatoms with respect to abiotic factors, a Generalized Linear Model (GLM) ANCOVA model was calculated
steps.searchable_summary: calculate GLM ANCOVA model for diatom diversity and uniqueness
inputs: Predictor variables (hydroperiod, substrate type, limnological data, geographical data)
outputs: GLM ANCOVA model object

steps.description: using hydroperiod and substrate type as categorical factors
steps.searchable_summary: specify hydroperiod and substrate type as categorical factors in GLM ANCOVA
outputs: GLM ANCOVA model specification with categorical factors

steps.description: and the other variables (limnological and geographical data) as continuous predictors.
steps.searchable_summary: specify limnological and geographical data as continuous predictors in GLM ANCOVA
outputs: GLM ANCOVA model specification with continuous predictors

steps.description: The resulting variables were selected based on a 'best subsets' algorithm (Neter et al., 1996).
steps.searchable_summary: select predictor variables using a best subsets algorithm
inputs: GLM ANCOVA model results for various variable combinations
outputs: Selected set of predictor variables for the final model

steps.description: To compare the performance of the different models that were generated, we used Akaike's Information Criterion (AIC).
steps.searchable_summary: compare model performance using Akaike's Information Criterion (AIC)
inputs: Multiple GLM ANCOVA models with different variable sets
outputs: AIC value for each generated model

steps.description: distance matrices were computed using appropriate metrics (Sorensen's index for floristic data, geographic distance for geographic data and Euclidean distance for elevational and limnological data)
steps.searchable_summary: compute distance matrices using specified metrics
outputs: Sorensen distance matrix (floristic)
Geographic distance matrix
Euclidean distance matrix (elevational)
Euclidean distance matrix (limnological)

steps.description: distance decay of similarity (DDS) was evaluated by means of Partial Mantel tests (Legendre & Legendre, 1998)
steps.searchable_summary: evaluate distance decay of similarity using Partial Mantel tests
outputs: Results of Partial Mantel tests (e.g., correlation coefficient, p-value)
